children.value: In the previous chapters, we explored how single qubits behave and how they can be manipulated using quantum gates. However, the most profound feature of quantum mechanics—and the one that provides the most power to quantum computers—is
Albert Einstein famously referred to entanglement as
While it seems paradoxical, it is a rigorous mathematical consequence of the tensor product structure of multi-qubit systems.

children.alt: Bell-pair diagram showing Alice and Bob sharing one entangled state with correlated measurement outcomes.

if it cannot be written as a product of the states of its individual qubits.

children.value: . In a separable state, measuring qubit A tells you nothing about the state of qubit B. They are independent.
If no such decomposition exists, the state is

. In an entangled state, the qubits share a collective identity: measuring one qubit fixes the correlated outcome for the other in the quantum description, regardless of how far apart they are. This correlation cannot be used to send information faster than light.
The most famous examples of entanglement are the four
(or EPR pairs). These are maximally entangled states of two qubits.

children.value: 5.3 The EPR Paradox and Bell’s Inequality

children.label: 5.3 The EPR Paradox and Bell’s Inequality

children.value: In 1935, Einstein, Podolsky, and Rosen (EPR) argued that quantum mechanics must be incomplete. They suggested there were “hidden variables”—pre-determined values—that decided the measurement outcomes, avoiding the need for “spooky action.”
In 1964, John Bell proved that no theory of local hidden variables could reproduce all the predictions of quantum mechanics. He derived
, which provide a mathematical limit on the correlations possible in any classical (local) system.
Quantum mechanics violates these inequalities. Experimental tests (like the CHSH experiment) have consistently ruled out local hidden-variable explanations of the observed correlations.
Entanglement allows us to move a quantum state from one location to another without physically moving the particle. This is
Alice wants to send an unknown state

children.value: Note: This does not allow faster-than-light communication because Alice must send the classical bits at the speed of light.
Entanglement isn’t limited to two qubits. The
(Greenberger-Horne-Zeilinger) is a maximally entangled state of three or more qubits:

children.html: ∣GHZ⟩=12(∣000⟩+∣111⟩)|GHZ\rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(|000\rangle + |111\rangle)∣GHZ⟩=2​1​(∣000⟩+∣111⟩)

children.value: GHZ states demonstrate “genuine tripartite entanglement,” where the correlation is shared across all three particles simultaneously.
Entanglement provides a way to build communication protocols whose security can be checked using quantum correlations. In
Alice and Bob share entangled pairs.
They measure them in random bases.
Because of Bell’s theorem, any attempt by an eavesdropper (Eve) to intercept the qubits will disturb the entanglement and introduce errors.
Alice and Bob can detect Eve simply by checking if Bell’s inequality is still violated.
